interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Dentist; Civil Rights Leader of the St. Augustine, Florida Movement (1963-65); Former NAACP Youth Council Advisor; U.S. Air Force Aeromedical Officer (Retired)
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill (on behalf of the Library of Congress)

main_summary.summary:
Dr. Robert B. Hayling, born November 20, 1929 in Tallahassee, Florida, recounts the arc of his life from a Florida A&M faculty family through Air Force service, dental training at Meharry Medical College, and his emergence as the central leader of the St. Augustine civil rights movement in 1963-64. Hayling describes his father as an Industrial Education instructor at Florida A&M specializing in tailoring (originally from Grenada), and his mother as a homemaker of Creole extraction from New Orleans. After graduating college he entered the Air Force in August 1951, trained at Lackland Air Force Base and Sheppard Air Force Base, and after Officer Candidate School (class 52D, December 1952) was assigned to Wright-Patterson Air Force Base in Dayton, Ohio as an aeromedical researcher in the Biomedical Laboratory, specializing in toxicology. He left the Air Force to begin dental school at Meharry in September 1955.

At Meharry, Hayling studied dental jurisprudence under Z. Alexander Looby, the lead attorney for desegregation of Tennessee public schools. When Looby's home was dynamited and blew out windows of Hayling's dormitory, Hayling participated in the silent April 19, 1960 march on Nashville City Hall, where he became acquainted with John Lewis, Diane Nash, C.T. Vivian, and James Bevel. After passing the Florida dental board in July 1960 and evaluating multiple Florida cities, he chose St. Augustine, inheriting the practice of the deceased Dr. Rudolph Gordon, whose largely white patient base he initially retained. He also served as dentist for the city jail, county jail, and state prison form.

In 1963, after Rev. Thomas A. Wright Sr. (the local NAACP Youth Council advisor) was forced to leave St. Augustine for Gainesville, a delegation of young people asked Hayling to take over. With NAACP chapter president Fannie Fulwood and secretary Mrs. Elizabeth Hawthorne, Hayling led picketing of segregated downtown establishments. When the city's 400th-anniversary celebration excluded Black citizens despite a federal $400,000 appropriation, Hayling wrote Vice President Lyndon B. Johnson; Johnson sent aide George Reedy to defuse the threat, after which Hayling and his delegation were seated in an alcove above the Ponce de Leon Hotel ballroom dinner and then "outfoxed" at a sham follow-up meeting attended only by the mayor's secretary and a tape recorder. In September 1963 Hayling and three companions were captured at a Ku Klux Klan rally headlined by Connie Lynch and J.B. Stoner, beaten with axe handles and baseball bats, and threatened with being burned alive in gasoline; an Episcopal priest from Daytona Beach who had infiltrated the rally with a personal recorder called the Tallahassee highway patrol and they were rescued and transported via mortician E.G. Chase's hearse to Brewster Hospital in Jacksonville. Hayling lost eleven teeth and sustained severe damage to his right arm. Klan leader Hoss Manucy, a convicted moonshiner, worked closely with Sheriff L.O. Davis, who deputized many Klan members as auxiliary sheriff's deputies.

After NAACP national president Roy Wilkins threatened to withdraw the St. Augustine chapter's charter for refusing to follow national instructions, Hayling preemptively mailed the charter back, then pivoted to SCLC through visits to Atlanta where he met Julian Bond and SNCC's "house mother" Ella Baker, with C.K. Steele facilitating connections to Dr. King's circle. Yale Divinity School students organized by Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr. came for spring break 1964 to tutor and support the movement, generating publicity that helped attract Massachusetts dignitaries: Mrs. Mary Peabody (mother of Governor Endicott "Chub" Peabody), Mrs. Esther Burgess (wife of Bishop John Melville Burgess, the first Black Episcopal bishop in the United States), Mrs. Rowe, and Mrs. Campbell. Hayling was arrested with Mary Peabody at the Monson Motor Lodge. ACLU attorney Tobias Simon of Miami provided legal aid. Hank Thomas, a St. Augustine native and Freedom Rider then attending an HBCU in North Carolina, was arrested for a one-man sit-in; word came that Sammy Davis Jr. had put up the $100 bond. James Brock, the Monson Motor Lodge owner, gained international notoriety for the June 18, 1964 photograph of him pouring acid into the motel pool where freedom demonstrators were swimming. St. Augustine demonstrations continued through the spring of 1964 until the Civil Rights Act of 1964 was signed; Hayling places Dr. King in St. Augustine on June 30, 1964.

After fulfilling the five-year service obligation tied to Florida's dental-school tuition supplement, Hayling moved his practice to Cocoa, Florida, near Cape Kennedy. On July 2, 2011, the City of St. Augustine presented him the Award of Valor at an ACCORD event where the Rev. Goldie Eubanks family was also honored and Dorothy Cotton (of SCLC) spoke. Scott Street in St. Augustine had been renamed "Dr. Hayling Place" on June 20, 2003. He closes with tribute to his neighbor Mrs. Barbara Edna Vickers and to Mrs. Maddie Jones, who served as his office assistant during the movement years.
key_themes: Black professional leadership in the civil rights movement
Military service as a path to civic claim
The St. Augustine campaign as catalyst for the Civil Rights Act of 1964
Split between local militancy and NAACP national gradualism
Cross-regional alliances (Yale, Massachusetts, SCLC) sustaining a local movement
Personal cost of activism: bodily injury, family disruption, professional risk
main_summary.historical_significance: Hayling's first-person testimony is a foundational primary source for the St. Augustine, Florida campaign of 1963-64, the immediate political pressure point that drove Senate cloture and final passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. He documents the local origin of the movement (a youth council without national sponsorship), the breakdown of relations with the NAACP national office, the pivot to SCLC, the September 1963 Klan beating that nearly killed him, and the international-press resonance of the Monson Motor Lodge acid-in-pool incident. The interview also preserves Hayling's Nashville-era ties to the Lawson-Lewis-Nash student movement and to Z. Alexander Looby, situating St. Augustine within the broader 1960-64 national civil rights chronology.

errors: The summary names 'Mrs. Rowe' and 'Mrs. Campbell' as Massachusetts supporters; the transcript identifies them only by surname and a generic description ('industrialist, millionaire') without first names, so these attributions are at the limit of what the transcript supports.

The summary attributes the dental-school supplement to 'Florida'; the transcript says the state paid $1,000/year supplement to Meharry for any Black Florida student attending dental school. The summary's paraphrase compresses this, which is acceptable but loses one specific.

The summary describes Hayling as captured 'with three companions' at the Klan rally; the transcript names them ambiguously through Whisper artifacts ('James Jackson of Mister house and James house and and clad Jenkins'), so the precise identity of the four men is not cleanly recoverable from the transcript alone. The summary does not invent the names but does generalize a count that the transcript itself states.

The summary identifies Hank Thomas as a student at 'an HBCU in North Carolina' rather than naming the school; Hayling himself says 'I'm going to say NC A&T but I would stand corrected if it's not but I know it was an end his name was Hank Thomas Hank Thomas,' so the summary's caution mirrors Hayling's own.
improvements: Tighten the wording around the four men beaten at the Klan rally to name them as the transcript permits (James Jackson and Clyde Jenkins are recoverable; the other two are Whisper-garbled).
Add explicit identification that the Florida tuition-supplement amount was $1,000 per year payable to Meharry.
Clarify that Hank Thomas's college affiliation is given as an uncertain identification by Hayling himself.

chapters.title: Tallahassee Childhood, Air Force Service, and Path to Dental School
chapters.summary:
Hayling opens by establishing his family roots in Tallahassee, Florida, where he was born November 20, 1929. He describes his father as an Industrial Education instructor at Florida A&M specializing in tailoring (originally from Grenada, requiring naturalization), and his mother as a homemaker of Creole extraction from New Orleans. Three siblings are named: an older brother Charles Hayling Jr., a deceased sister Frances Hayling, and youngest sister Yvonne Hayling Clark of Sarasota. All four children attended Florida A&M and pursued advanced degrees.

In August 1951 Hayling entered the U.S. Air Force as a college graduate, trained at Lackland Air Force Base in San Antonio, then in a medical-technician program at Sheppard Air Force Base in Wichita Falls, Texas. He served on hospital staff and was sponsored for Officer Candidate School, graduating in class 52D in December 1952 and commissioning as a second lieutenant. He was assigned to Wright-Patterson Air Force Base in Dayton, Ohio as an aeromedical researcher in the Biomedical Laboratory, with toxicology as his department field. He describes being offered an early promotion to captain to remain in the Air Force but chose to leave for dental school. In the interim he married his college sweetheart (a Miami school teacher) and brought her to Dayton; she would later return to Miami before he matriculated at Meharry Medical College in September 1955.
related_events: U.S. Air Force Officer Candidate School (class 52D, December 1952)
Florida A&M University
Meharry Medical College
Wright-Patterson Air Force Base Biomedical Laboratory
Sheppard Air Force Base medical-technician program

improvements: Could explicitly note that Hayling identifies his father's tenure at Florida A&M as '33 years' as a personal compromise between the '32 or 34' he heard from others.

chapters.title: Meharry, Z. Alexander Looby, and the Nashville Student Movement
chapters.summary:
Hayling describes his Meharry years (1955-onward) and his peer cohort, including future Meharry dental-school dean Fred Fielder. He was aware of Nashville civil rights activity as a student but not intimately involved until his dental jurisprudence instructor Z. Alexander Looby - lead attorney for desegregation of Tennessee public schools - had his home dynamited in 1960. The blast blew out windows of Hayling's dormitory; he served as a marshal helping with glass cleanup. Hayling participated in the April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall to protest the bombing, becoming acquainted with John Lewis, Diane Nash, C.T. Vivian, and James Bevel (noting that Bevel and Nash later married, divorced, and had children).

Hayling describes this as his first active role in a civil rights demonstration, though he also references Tallahassee movements led by Rev. C.K. Steele (the bus desegregation campaign that bankrupted the Tallahassee bus system) and by Patricia Stephens Due and Priscilla Stephens. He notes that a life-size monument of C.K. Steele now stands at the Tallahassee bus station, and that Patricia Stephens Due and her husband John Due (a young attorney who later helped in St. Augustine) co-wrote 'Freedom in the Family.'
related_events: Bombing of Z. Alexander Looby's home (April 1960)
April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall
Tallahassee Bus Boycott (Rev. C.K. Steele)
Nashville sit-in movement
Patricia Stephens Due and Priscilla Stephens jail incarceration in Tallahassee

errors: The transcript shows Hayling pausing on 'La Theat, I have to get La Theat's first name' - this is almost certainly Bernard Lafayette but the transcript's own uncertainty is preserved by omitting the name from the chapter summary.
improvements: Could add Hayling's own framing that he was 'aware...but not intimately involved' as a Meharry student, to distinguish his Nashville role from later St. Augustine leadership.

chapters.title: Choosing St. Augustine and Building a Dental Practice
chapters.summary:
Hayling describes his transition to professional life. After taking the Florida dental board exam over the July 4, 1960 weekend, he toured the state in his Volkswagen convertible evaluating cities. He chose St. Augustine because the practice and office facilities of the deceased Dr. Rudolph Gordon - a charismatic dentist whose practice was '50 to 60 or 60 to 70 percent white' - had become available through Mrs. Gordon. A December 1960 housewarming opened the practice. Hayling notes that even in St. Augustine he would drive to Jacksonville at night to find 'civilization,' as the town 'rolled up the sidewalks at sundown.'

Hayling also held appointments as dentist for the city jail, county jail, and state prison form for St. Johns County and the City of St. Augustine, blocking off scheduling periods so that armed escort officers would not encounter other patients. He describes private dental treatment for many sheriff's deputies and for Sheriff L.O. Davis himself, frequently on the house. He clarifies under questioning that there was no coercive arrangement, but that he was not yet 'radioactive' from civil rights activity at this stage. He also notes that St. Augustine had only one Black deputy sheriff and one Black officer (Freed Waters) who patrolled the Black community of Lincolnville at night in civilian clothes with a pistol.
related_events: Florida dental board examination (July 1960)
Dr. Gordon dental practice succession
St. Johns County jail and state prison-form dental service
Lincolnville Black community policing

improvements: The transcript names Dr. Gordon's first name as 'Rudolph' indirectly; the summary's identification could be flagged as inferential rather than stated.

chapters.title: Taking Up the Youth Council Movement and the 400th-Anniversary Confrontation
chapters.summary:
Hayling explains that his entry into movement leadership was unstructured. The previous local NAACP Youth Council advisor, Rev. Thomas A. Wright Sr., a Baptist minister and Florida Memorial College instructor in St. Augustine, was forced out by establishment pressure: his wife (an elementary school teacher) was fired, and he relocated to Gainesville, where he became pastor of Mount Carmel Baptist Church. His oldest daughter became the first Black student to integrate the Gainesville, Florida high school. A delegation of youth then asked Hayling to become their advisor, which he accepted. The initial activity consisted of disciplined picketing and demonstrating downtown.

Hayling worked closely with the local NAACP chapter president Fannie Fulwood and secretary Mrs. Elizabeth Hawthorne (who rode a bicycle across town despite a 'hunchback' deformity), with Ruby Hurley as the Atlanta-based regional NAACP director. Hayling describes how the planning for St. Augustine's 400th anniversary - with a federal $400,000 appropriation and the inclusion of an ambassador from Spain, the vice president, and other dignitaries - excluded all Black citizens despite a roughly 20 percent Black population and the presence of Black principals and the college president Dr. Perrier. Hayling wrote Vice President Lyndon B. Johnson protesting the segregated dinner; Johnson sent aide George Reedy a week ahead of the dinner. Hayling and 'six or eight' attendees were ultimately seated in an alcove above the Ponce de Leon Hotel ballroom, with only Secret Service and FBI agents as their white tablemates. The next morning's promised meeting with the City Commission and Mayor proved to be a sham: only the Mayor's secretary and a tape recorder were present. As a further deception, the establishment dressed Lincolnville's Black night-patrol officer in a police uniform and stationed him in uniform at the Old Slave Market downtown so the press could be told that the city did employ a Black policeman.
related_events: St. Augustine 400th-anniversary celebration dinner
$400,000 federal commemoration appropriation
Vice President Lyndon B. Johnson's intervention via George Reedy
Florida Memorial College in St. Augustine
Rev. Thomas A. Wright Sr. forced relocation to Gainesville

improvements: Could note explicitly that the 'biracial committee' was the central concrete demand on paper, alongside opportunities for Black applicants to take the fireman and police examinations.

chapters.title: Allies and the Internal Strategy of the Movement
chapters.summary:
Hayling credits a network of supporters: Mr. Henry Twine, Mr. Oscar Turner, and Mr. Clyde Jenkins among the six-to-eight adult spokespersons who attended meetings; the Elks members who made the second floor of the 'Elks rest' available for meetings and press setups; and a restaurant across the street from his office owned by 'Mr. Pennington' (a 'world famous' ice-cream maker) which served any demonstrator and submitted meal tabs to Hayling for reimbursement. He also describes Hank Thomas, a St. Augustine native then attending an HBCU in North Carolina (Hayling identifies it tentatively as NC A&T) and a Freedom Rider, who staged a one-man sit-in at a local drug store and was arrested with a $100 bond. The local NAACP word was that Sammy Davis Jr. had put up the bond money; Hayling notes Thomas later became an entrepreneur owning McDonald's and Marriott franchises in Atlanta.

Hayling describes pressure from above and below: the establishment instructed Florida Memorial College faculty and Black school teachers not to participate; many supported financially but not publicly. College students who joined were threatened by their instructors with denial of make-up work and exam access until Hayling raised the prospect of a lawsuit. ACLU attorney Tobias Simon of Miami served as the movement's lead legal aid; Hayling reports that Simon ran the Boston Marathon credibly and died of a heart attack a day or two after one of his races.

Hayling also recounts his admission as the first African-American dentist to the local county chapter of the American Dental Association with full membership and privileges, and how the planned officer rotation that would have placed him in the leadership line was averted when the dental association cancelled the relevant meeting after he arrived; subsequent meetings were quietly held without notifying his office.
related_events: Hank Thomas one-man sit-in and arrest
Tobias Simon ACLU legal aid
Florida Memorial College faculty suppression
American Dental Association local chapter integration

errors: Hayling tentatively places Hank Thomas at NC A&T but disclaims certainty in the transcript; the summary should preserve that uncertainty (it does).
improvements: Could highlight Hayling's explicit framing of the moral arc: he and Mrs. Maddie Jones and Mrs. Hawthorne stayed when others were afraid he was 'like a leper.'

chapters.title: The September 1963 Klan Beating
chapters.summary:
Hayling recounts the night the local Klan held a major rally on US-1 outside St. Augustine, featuring Connie Lynch from California as the main speaker and J.B. Stoner from Atlanta. Hayling and three other men (named in the transcript through Whisper-garbled forms but including James Jackson and Clyde Jenkins) parked along US-1 to observe. Klansmen with guns surrounded their car. A side-road escape attempt was blocked by a ditch the Klan had dug across the road. The men were dragged out, their wallets and identification taken; the discovery of Hayling's NAACP card escalated the beating to severe force with axe handles and baseball bats. The four were piled semi-conscious on the rally stage 'like cordwood.'

An Episcopal priest from Daytona Beach had infiltrated the rally with a personal recorder. As the violence escalated and as the announcer offered the crowd the 'privilege' of smelling 'niggers burn' and indicated gasoline had been sent for, the priest left the rally, found a pay phone, and called the Tallahassee highway patrol. State authorities reached Sheriff L.O. Davis and outside law enforcement began arriving. The men were rescued and transported first to Flagler Hospital's emergency room, then via local mortician E.G. Chase's hearse - serving as an ad hoc ambulance - to the Black-staffed Brewster Hospital in Jacksonville for full treatment.

Hayling lost eleven teeth and sustained severe damage to his right arm; he notes that the assailants seemed to deliberately target the right arm of a right-handed dentist. He describes Klan leader Hoss Manucy as a convicted moonshiner who had served time in federal prison, and notes that Sheriff L.O. Davis deputized many Klan members as auxiliary deputies. Hayling rejects the framing that the beating made him a 'rebel' or justified a 'so-called militancy'; rather, he frames the experience against his expectation that Air Force service should have secured full citizenship.
related_events: September 1963 Klan rally outside St. Augustine
Klan beating of Hayling, James Jackson, Clyde Jenkins, and a fourth man
Sheriff L.O. Davis deputization of Klan members
Brewster Hospital (Jacksonville) emergency treatment

errors: The four men's names are partially obscured by Whisper artifacts in the source transcript; the summary names only the two recoverable identifications (Jackson, Jenkins) and notes a fourth man.
improvements: Could foreground the Episcopal priest's role more clearly as the rescue trigger; the transcript treats this as an incidental detail but it was decisive.

chapters.title: Break with the NAACP and Pivot to SCLC
chapters.summary:
Hayling describes the deterioration of his relationship with NAACP national leadership in late 1963. Roy Wilkins, the national president, called repeatedly from New York to instruct St. Augustine on movement strategy. After Hayling refused to take instruction from above, Wilkins called and indicated the NAACP was prepared to withdraw the chapter's charter. Hayling preemptively closed the conversation by asking whether Wilkins would be in his office on Saturday; he then mailed the charter back to New York via special delivery with return receipt through Mrs. Elizabeth Hawthorne, resigning before the chapter could be expelled.

With St. Augustine 'up the creek without a paddle,' Hayling made weekend trips to Atlanta to build alternative ties. He visited Julian Bond and the SNCC office, met Ella Baker (whom he calls the 'house mother' of SNCC), and worked his way into connections with Ebenezer Baptist Church and Daddy King. C.K. Steele, then in SCLC's board leadership, helped facilitate connections. Hayling explains that no national organization wanted to adopt 'a trouble making chapter and a renegade group'; SNCC was absorbed in Mississippi work and SCLC was depleted after Birmingham and other campaigns. He explicitly references the St. Augustine chapter's refusal to honor the cessation-of-demonstrations pause that Lyndon Johnson requested of Dr. King and other organizations during the 1964 Civil Rights Act passage push, declaring 'fool me once, shame on you, fool me twice, shame on me' and continuing demonstrations until the Act was signed. He notes that Dr. King was in St. Augustine on June 30, 1964 and that the Act was signed (he later confirms July 2, 1964) shortly after.
related_events: Roy Wilkins charter-withdrawal call
Resignation of St. Augustine NAACP chapter charter
Hayling's Atlanta visits to SNCC and SCLC
Cessation-of-demonstrations request from Lyndon B. Johnson
Civil Rights Act of 1964

improvements: Could explicitly preserve Hayling's self-correction: he had said publicly that King flew from St. Augustine to Washington for the Act signing, but in this interview corrects himself, noting only that King was in St. Augustine June 30, 1964.

chapters.title: Yale Spring Break, Mary Peabody, and the Monson Motor Lodge
chapters.summary:
Hayling describes Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr. (of Yale Divinity School) and 'Rev. Robinson' organizing Yale students to spend their spring break in St. Augustine doing tutorial work and clean-up rather than going to the beach. He calls it 'a publicity dream that we never could have concocted on our own.' Many Black St. Augustine families housed the Yale volunteers in their homes, and the students later staged a drama based on their experience in New Haven, with characters portraying L.O. Davis and Judge Charles Mathis. Influential Yale parents subsequently sent financial support.

Hayling describes the Massachusetts contingent that came to St. Augustine: Mrs. Mary Peabody (mother of sitting Massachusetts Governor Endicott 'Chub' Peabody, then 72 years old), Mrs. Esther Burgess (wife of Bishop John Melville Burgess, the first Black Episcopal bishop in the United States), Mrs. Rowe and Mrs. Campbell (both wives of industrialist millionaires). Jackie Robinson came to speak at a rally but did not consent to be arrested. Hayling was arrested with Mary Peabody at the Monson Motor Lodge restaurant - which, the interviewer notes, has since been demolished and replaced by the Bayfront Hilton in which the interview is being conducted.

Hayling then turns to James Brock, the Monson Motor Lodge owner, describing the 'infamous picture' of Brock pouring acid into the swimming pool with freedom demonstrators in the water, which 'went all the way around the world.' Hayling notes that Brock 'went to his grave an unrequited, uncompromised segregationist,' though Brock's daughter (whom Hayling has not met personally) has reportedly told others that her high-school experience with Black students changed her view from her father's.
related_events: Yale Divinity School spring-break tutorial program in St. Augustine
Arrest of Mary Peabody at the Monson Motor Lodge
James Brock acid-in-pool incident
Bishop John Melville Burgess (first Black Episcopal bishop in the U.S.)

errors: The 'Rev. Robinson' co-named with Coffin is not identified beyond a surname in the transcript; the summary preserves that limitation by giving only the surname.
improvements: Could add that the Monson Motor Lodge acid-in-pool image is dated June 18, 1964 per the ground-truth corpus, though the transcript itself does not state the date.

chapters.title: Departure to Cocoa, Florida and the Award of Valor
chapters.summary:
Hayling describes the conditions of his post-1964 departure. Florida's tuition supplement to Meharry obligated him to five years of service in a designated need area; St. Augustine satisfied that requirement. After fulfilling the obligation he moved to Cocoa, Florida, near Cape Kennedy, opening a practice in an Elks building next to physician Dr. Jerkins. His practice grew quickly through Cape Kennedy workers, technicians, and NASA scientists. He notes Lyndon B. Johnson's later relocation of NASA's mission control payroll to the Johnson Space Center as a setback to the Cocoa economy. His family dentist in Tallahassee, Dr. Campbell, had offered to give Hayling his Tallahassee practice, but Hayling chose Cocoa for the younger, more educated client base. He bought a two-bedroom house in Cocoa and intended to join it to an adjacent three-bedroom house but was barred by a one-water-meter-per-house code.

He credits his wife - a school teacher with a master's degree in biology - with raising the children to be 'good scholars' through the upheavals. His youngest daughter is a Yale graduate and Stanford MBA holder; her husband Chris is a Princeton and Harvard Business School graduate working for Apple Inc. in the Far East (based in Singapore at the time); together they brought grandsons Roman (then 8) and Avery (then 6) to St. Augustine on July 2, 2011 for the City of St. Augustine's presentation of the Award of Valor to Hayling. The Rev. Goldie Eubanks family was also honored at the ACCORD event, where Dorothy Cotton (of SCLC) spoke. Hayling's older daughter and her husband traveled from Mississippi; his youngest sister Yvonne came from Sarasota.
related_events: Move to Cocoa, Florida
Cape Kennedy / Johnson Space Center workforce shift
City of St. Augustine Award of Valor (July 2, 2011)
ACCORD ceremony with Rev. Goldie Eubanks family

improvements: Could clarify that Hayling explicitly identifies July 2, 2011 as the 47th anniversary of the Civil Rights Act.

chapters.title: Dr. Hayling Place and Tributes to the Foot Soldiers
chapters.summary:
Hayling closes with two acts of recognition. First, he describes the June 20, 2003 ceremony at which Scott Street in St. Augustine was renamed 'Dr. Hayling Place.' His daughter brought his then-seven-month-old grandson Roman from California (the family was living in San Mateo at the time). Commissioner Earl Jones, whose mother Mrs. Maddie Jones had been Hayling's office assistant for three or more years, championed honoring Hayling 'while he was still able to smell the roses.' Hayling pays particular tribute to Mrs. Maddie Jones, who stayed at the office through the period when many considered Hayling 'like a leper... that my office would possibly be shot up or dynamited.'

Second, prompted by the interviewer, Hayling pays tribute to Mrs. Barbara Edna Vickers, his neighbor from across the street on the road later renamed for him. He references his ongoing work with ACCORD and the Freedom Trail, and lifts the street-name sign for the camera as part of a ceremonial routine he uses to involve other movement participants in recounting how they earned the recognition. The interview closes with the standard Library of Congress / Smithsonian NMAAHC sign-off.
related_events: Renaming of Scott Street to Dr. Hayling Place (June 20, 2003)
Tribute to Mrs. Maddie Jones
Tribute to Mrs. Barbara Edna Vickers
ACCORD Freedom Trail

improvements: Could note that the sign-handling routine is described as a recurring practice in subsequent settings, not a one-time ceremony.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Dentist; Civil Rights Leader of the St. Augustine, Florida Movement (1963-65); Former NAACP Youth Council Advisor; U.S. Air Force Aeromedical Officer (Retired)
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill (on behalf of the Library of Congress)

evidence: Chronological opening establishing Tallahassee family, Florida A&M, Air Force, Meharry (Segments 17-78, 00:01:35,000)
Clear pivot from Nashville student movement to St. Augustine practice to movement leadership (Segments 117-216, 00:15:50,000)
Closing arc returning to recognition and tribute (Segments 476-497, 01:46:58,000)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Mosnier's questions create a strong chronological-then-thematic spine. Hayling does meander into long ancillary anecdotes (the New York summer waiter job at Segment 140-151, the office practice details at Segment 178-200) but recovers via clear interviewer redirects. The interview has a satisfying close with the Award of Valor and street-naming bookend.

most_vivid_moment.quote: We were piled on top of each other semi conscious like cordwood on top of each other on the stage

evidence: Specific date of birth (November 20, 1929) and dental school start (September 1955) (Segment 18, 00:01:44,000; Segment 70, 00:09:36,000)
Specific Officer Candidate School class designation (52D, December 1952) (Segment 46, 00:05:27,000)
Klan rally violence with axe handles and baseball bats and announcer's 'privilege of smelling a nigger burn' (Segments 343-348, 01:13:04,000-01:15:51,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Hayling provides exceptional name, date, and place specificity throughout - including full names of obscure local figures like Mrs. Maddie Jones, Mr. Pennington, E.G. Chase, and Dr. Perrier. The Klan beating sequence is particularly cinematic with sensory and procedural detail.

most_authentic_moment.quote: the injuries I encountered I would take to my grief

vulnerability_elements: Acknowledging he was 'outfoxed' multiple times by the establishment
Recognizing the personal cost of activism in terms of lost teeth and a damaged right arm
Reflecting on his belief that Air Force service should have secured citizenship and the disappointment when it did not
emotional_authenticity.justification: Hayling speaks with steady, dignified emotion rather than dramatic outburst. His most vulnerable moments are quiet: the acknowledgement that injuries 'I would take to my grave,' the recognition that his Air Force service did not secure his citizenship rights, and his explicit refusal to use the beating as justification for 'militancy.'

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Hayling delivers a sustained, detailed, and emotionally controlled narrative across 1:54. The interviewer's chronological scaffolding keeps the structure clear. The narrative gains its greatest force in the September 1963 Klan beating sequence and the moment-by-moment recounting of the NAACP charter resignation. Some episodes (the New York waiter job, the dental association ladder) are reported in more procedural detail than narrative urgency.

major_events: April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall after Looby bombing (Segment 114, 00:14:51,000)
Resignation of St. Augustine NAACP charter (Segments 365-377, 01:22:02,000-01:25:21,000)
Yale Divinity School spring break 1964 and Massachusetts dignitary arrests (Segments 400-436, 01:30:29,000-01:38:24,000)

key_figures.name: Roy Wilkins
key_figures.relationship: NAACP national president; called Hayling repeatedly demanding strategic compliance

key_figures.name: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
key_figures.relationship: SCLC leader Hayling recruited to St. Augustine; met fleetingly at a Tallahassee rally

key_figures.name: Ella Baker
key_figures.relationship: SNCC mentor met during Atlanta visits

key_figures.name: Mary Peabody
key_figures.relationship: Co-arrestee at the Monson Motor Lodge

key_figures.name: Sheriff L.O. Davis
key_figures.relationship: Local antagonist; deputized Klan members; was a private dental patient

organizational_involvement.role: Youth Council advisor and de facto chapter leader
Local affiliate / coordinator for the St. Augustine campaign

organizational_involvement.organization: American Dental Association (St. Johns County chapter)

organizational_involvement.role: Continuing-engagement role at time of interview

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Hayling held the central leadership role in the St. Augustine, Florida civil rights campaign, the local pressure point that drove Senate cloture on the Civil Rights Act of 1964. He is the canonical first-person witness to the Sept 1963 Klan beating, the NAACP charter break, and the recruitment of Dr. King to St. Augustine. His Meharry years also placed him at the periphery of the Nashville student movement.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Hayling occupies the single most consequential local-leader role in one of the four or five most-cited national civil rights campaigns of 1963-64. His firsthand testimony is exceptionally well-positioned in both the Nashville and St. Augustine arcs and his decisions (resigning NAACP charter, pivoting to SCLC, refusing the cessation pause) directly shaped national outcomes.

precision_examples: April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall (Segment 114, 00:14:51,000)
$400,000 federal appropriation for the St. Augustine 400th anniversary (Segment 237, 00:43:16,000)
June 30, 1964 confirmation of Dr. King's presence in St. Augustine (Segment 393, 01:29:22,000)
June 20, 2003 street naming and July 2, 2011 Award of Valor presentation (Segments 487-488, 01:50:40,000-01:51:08,000; Segment 476, 01:46:58,000)
verifiable_claims: Air Force entry August 1951; OCS class 52D December 1952
Meharry dental school class entering September 1955
Z. Alexander Looby home bombing and April 19, 1960 silent march
St. Augustine NAACP chapter charter resigned 1963
Mary Peabody arrest at the Monson Motor Lodge spring 1964
Civil Rights Act of 1964 signed July 2, 1964
Scott Street renamed Dr. Hayling Place June 20, 2003
specificity_of_details.justification: Date and name specificity is exceptional throughout, with Hayling repeatedly self-correcting or flagging when he is uncertain (e.g., NC A&T for Hank Thomas, the exact King-departure-to-Washington claim). This metacognitive precision is itself characteristic of high-grade primary-source testimony.

new_information: The role of the Episcopal priest from Daytona Beach as the rescue trigger for the Klan beating
Mrs. Maddie Jones's role as a dental office assistant who stayed when Hayling was 'like a leper'
The Mr. Pennington restaurant feeding subsidy as a logistical infrastructure for demonstrators
The mechanics of the dental association rotation that was used to block Hayling from leadership
understudied_aspects: Black professional class as movement leadership in mid-sized Southern cities
Internal NAACP national-local conflict that pushed local chapters toward SCLC
Behind-the-scenes infrastructure (food, meeting space, legal aid) of a campaign without national funding
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Hayling's account complicates the standard SCLC-led narrative of St. Augustine by foregrounding the local youth-council origin, the NAACP rupture, and the explicit refusal of the Johnson-requested cessation of demonstrations. He also complicates the lone-Dr.-King narrative of the Civil Rights Act passage by documenting the local pressure that made St. Augustine indispensable.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of the St. Augustine campaign (Colburn, Warren, and others) rely on Hayling's testimony as primary source. The interview confirms and adds detail to the Klan beating, NAACP break, and Yale spring-break elements. The Mrs. Maddie Jones tribute and the dental-association-rotation episode are likely new contributions even within the existing Hayling literature.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Hayling's interview adds substantial new texture to a well-studied campaign, with several first-time-on-record contributions (the priest rescue mechanism, the Pennington restaurant subsidy, the dental association maneuver). The campaign itself is well-covered but Hayling's distinctive voice and the late-life detail he offers fill specific gaps.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Hayling delivers exceptional historical value: insider testimony at the center of a campaign that produced the Civil Rights Act of 1964, with precise dates, full names, and verifiable claims. The interview adds new texture to a known story (the priest rescue, the dental-association maneuver, the Mrs. Maddie Jones role) and clarifies points where Hayling's earlier public statements were imprecise (the King-to-Washington claim).

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Hayling describes a journey from a young Air Force officer who believed military service would secure full citizenship, through a returning veteran/dentist who initially treated white sheriff's deputies on the house and was 'a good fellow' before becoming 'radioactive,' to a movement leader willing to mail back the NAACP charter rather than take instruction from above. The arc is one of expanding moral clarity rather than dramatic conversion.
key_turning_points: Z. Alexander Looby bombing and April 19, 1960 silent march (Segments 96-116, 00:12:40,000-00:15:42,000)
Youth delegation asking him to take over from Rev. Wright (Segments 216-217, 00:37:53,000-00:38:29,000)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Before: Air Force officer, dentist building a private practice, expecting that professional achievement and military service would secure full rights. After: nationally-known movement leader who resigned NAACP charter, was beaten nearly to death, and stayed in Florida through five years of obligated service before relocating to rebuild.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Highly inspirational in a quiet register - Hayling's refusal to frame the beating as a militancy-trigger, and his closing tribute to the unsung Mrs. Maddie Jones and Mrs. Hawthorne, model an ethic of leadership grounded in service rather than self-promotion.
transformation_growth.justification: The transformation arc is clear, well-grounded in specific turning points, and morally substantive. Hayling explicitly refuses the dramatic-conversion framing that interviewers often offer, which is itself a form of growth narrative.

peak_moments.description: Captured at the Klan rally, surrounded by armed Klansmen, beaten with axe handles and baseball bats, threatened with being burned alive in gasoline
peak_moments.stakes: Mortal danger - the announcer explicitly stated the intent to douse the four men with gasoline and burn them alive

peak_moments.description: Outfoxed by the establishment at the morning-after meeting following the 400th-anniversary dinner - arriving in Sunday-go-to-meeting clothes to find only the Mayor's secretary and a tape recorder
peak_moments.stakes: Public humiliation; perceived loss of moral and political leverage

peak_moments.description: Roy Wilkins call - Hayling preemptively resigns the NAACP charter rather than be expelled
peak_moments.stakes: Loss of national affiliation, financial isolation, organizational orphan status

conflict_types: Political deception (sham morning meeting; uniformed Lincolnville officer)
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From dignified anger and grief over the beating to wry humor over being 'outfoxed' to quiet pride in mailing the charter back
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: The interview has multiple high-stakes peaks, with the Klan beating sequence as the dramatic centerpiece. Conflicts span the physical, institutional, political, and professional registers. The 'fool me twice' line in the SCLC/cessation context is a clear triumph moment.

universal_themes: Marrying a college sweetheart and the disruption of professional moves on family
The vulnerability of physical injury and rehabilitation
Recognition late in life from a hometown that once turned away
relatable_struggles: Building a professional practice from nothing in an unfamiliar city
Standing up to institutional authority that demands compliance
Watching a family member (his wife) sacrifice career to support a relocation
human_moments: Driving to Jacksonville at night to find 'civilization' because St. Augustine 'rolled up the sidewalks at sundown' (Segment 167, 00:27:41,000)
Sending his daughter to play with her father's dog Mandana in their Dayton apartment (Segments 62-64, 00:08:22,000-00:08:50,000)
Lifting his street-name sign for the camera in a ceremonial routine he uses with other movement participants (Segments 495-496, 01:53:13,000-01:53:45,000)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: The veterans' citizenship theme, the family-disruption-from-career-relocation theme, and the late-life recognition theme are universally accessible. The Klan-beating recovery story has the universal valence of a survivor's account. Some institutional details (NAACP politics, dental-association rotation) require some context.
relatable_experiences.justification: The interview is moderately relatable - universal themes are present but mediated through quite specific institutional and procedural detail. The most relatable moments are the quiet asides about family, marriage, and recognition rather than the high-drama public moments.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Hayling's narrative has substantial emotional resonance grounded in physical danger, institutional defiance, and moral clarity. The transformation arc is genuine and clearly articulated; the dramatic peaks are vivid; the relatability is moderate. The interview's emotional power is cumulative rather than peak-driven.

barriers: Occasional procedural detail (dental association rotation, Florida tuition supplement) requires brief context for non-specialist audiences
Some sentences run long and embed multiple clauses, which may require a careful listen
language_clarity.justification: Hayling speaks in clear, formal, complete sentences. He frequently self-defines terms ('OCS,' 'NAACP regional director,' 'SCLC'). Some sentences are long and embed multiple clauses, but rarely confusingly so. Vocabulary is professional but accessible.

high_energy_sections: Klan rally and beating sequence (Segments 335-356, 01:09:28,000-01:18:34,000) - cinematic detail and rising stakes
Roy Wilkins charter call and Hayling's preemptive resignation (Segments 370-377, 01:23:45,000-01:25:21,000) - clean confrontation
slow_sections: New York summer waiter job recounting (Segments 140-152, 00:21:59,000-00:25:03,000) - tangential to the civil rights spine
Detailed dental-practice administrative recounting (Segments 178-200, 00:30:04,000-00:33:59,000) - procedural rather than narrative
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Most audiences can sustain attention across the 1:54 length given the interviewer's strong chronological scaffolding, with potential drift during the longer procedural sections in the middle portion.
narrative_flow.justification: Mosnier's questioning maintains forward momentum, with explicit returns ('Let me bring you back to...') when Hayling drifts. The Klan beating sequence and the NAACP resignation provide strong narrative peaks. The waiter-job and dental-practice procedural digressions slow momentum but do not derail it.

themes_connected: Veterans' citizenship expectations and the gap between military service and civil rights
Local-vs-national tensions in the civil rights movement
Black professional class as a leadership reservoir under threat
The Civil Rights Act of 1964 as the policy outcome of accumulated local pressure
Late-life memorialization and the Freedom Trail movement
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Hayling explicitly connects his Air Force service to a civic claim that the country failed to honor, a theme that resonates with contemporary discussions of equity, veteran experience, and the meaning of citizenship. The Award of Valor and street-naming sections explicitly thematize the work of late memorialization (ACCORD, Freedom Trail).
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Hayling explicitly draws meaning from his experience throughout, including his refusal to frame the beating as a militancy-trigger and his closing tribute to the unsung 'foot soldiers' (Mrs. Maddie Jones, Mrs. Hawthorne, the Eubanks family).
broader_themes_connection.justification: Hayling provides strong, explicit connections from personal experience to broader movement themes and contemporary meaning. The ACCORD/Freedom Trail framing at the close ties the personal narrative to ongoing public memory work.

accessibility.overall_assessment: The interview is highly accessible for general audiences within the constraints of a 1:54 oral history. Hayling's articulation is excellent, his vocabulary professional but accessible, his explicit thematic framing strong. The procedural-detail digressions are the main accessibility risk for casual listeners.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: This is a Smithsonian-grade primary source from the central living leader of the St. Augustine, Florida civil rights campaign - one of the four or five most-cited national civil rights flashpoints of 1963-64. Hayling delivers precise dates, full names, and verifiable claims, with metacognitive markers when he is uncertain. The narrative has a strong chronological-thematic spine, multiple high-drama peaks (the Sept 1963 Klan beating, the NAACP charter resignation), and a satisfying late-life recognition arc. Procedural digressions slow some middle sections but do not undermine overall coherence. The interview's institutional density (NAACP, SCLC, SNCC, ACLU, ADA, Yale Divinity, ACCORD) is exceptional and is offered with sufficient context for general audiences.
key_strengths: Exceptional name, date, and place specificity throughout, with explicit self-correction when uncertain (e.g., the King-to-Washington flight, Hank Thomas's college affiliation)
First-person testimony at the center of a campaign that drove Civil Rights Act passage, with insider perspective on the NAACP/SCLC pivot and the dignitary-recruitment infrastructure
Quiet moral clarity in the framing of the Klan beating and the NAACP resignation - Hayling explicitly refuses the militancy framing that interviewers often offer
key_limitations: Several middle-section procedural digressions (New York waiter job, dental-practice administration, dental association rotation) reduce narrative momentum without adding civil-rights-historical value

The four men captured at the Klan rally are not all cleanly identifiable from the transcript alone due to Whisper artifacts in the source SRT; only James Jackson and Clyde Jenkins are recoverable with confidence

claims.claim: Hayling was born November 20, 1929 in Tallahassee, Florida
claims.supporting_excerpt: Yes. I was born in Tallahassee, Florida, November 20, 1929. (Segment 18, 00:01:44,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of date and place of birth.

claims.claim: Hayling's father was an Industrial Education instructor at Florida A&M specializing in tailoring, originally from Grenada
claims.supporting_excerpt: My father was an instructor on the faculty of Florida A&M, which was college at that time in the Industrial Education Department. His specialty was tailoring... My father was from Grenada. (Segments 19-23, 00:01:44,000-00:02:26,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of father's role, specialty, institution, and national origin.

claims.claim: Hayling's mother was a homemaker of Creole extraction from New Orleans, Louisiana
claims.supporting_excerpt: And my mother, for many, many years in our developmental years, was a homemaker. But she was out of New Orleans, Louisiana, of Creole extraction. (Segments 21-22, 00:02:03,000-00:02:14,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of mother's role, origin, and Creole identity.

claims.claim: Three siblings: Charles Hayling Jr. (older), Frances Hayling (deceased), Yvonne Hayling Clark (Sarasota)
claims.supporting_excerpt: There were four children in the family. I have an older brother, Charles Hayling Jr. ... I had a sister who is deceased now, Francis Hayling. And then my youngest sister, Yvonne Hayling Clark, is now retired living in Sarasota, Florida. (Segments 27-31, 00:02:50,000-00:03:14,000)
claims.rationale: Direct enumeration of siblings, with names, status, and current residence for the youngest.

claims.claim: Hayling entered the Air Force in August 1951 and graduated Officer Candidate School in class 52D, December 1952
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was fortunate enough to go into the Air Force as a college graduate on August of 1951. ... commissioned as a second lieutenant in the class of 52D, which was December of 1952. (Segments 35, 46, 00:03:43,000, 00:05:27,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person dates and class designation.

claims.claim: Hayling was assigned to Wright-Patterson Air Force Base in Dayton, Ohio as an aeromedical researcher in the Biomedical Laboratory specializing in toxicology
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was assigned to an extremely good assignment of Rev. Thomas A. Wright Sr Patterson Air Force Base Daytono Hyle, as a Aeromedical Researcher and the Biomedical Laboratory that I was assigned to. And my department field was toxicology. (Segments 47-49, 00:05:41,000-00:06:10,000)
claims.rationale: Whisper has garbled 'Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, Dayton, Ohio' to 'Rev. Thomas A. Wright Sr Patterson Air Force Base Daytono Hyle'; the assignment to Wright-Patterson and the toxicology specialty are clearly stated.

claims.claim: Hayling began dental school at Meharry Medical College in September 1955
claims.supporting_excerpt: everything was so school started in 55, September. And at Meharry. (Segments 70-71, 00:09:36,000-00:09:38,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of school start date and institution.

claims.claim: Z. Alexander Looby was Hayling's dental jurisprudence instructor and the lead attorney for desegregation of Tennessee public schools
claims.supporting_excerpt: my jurisprudence or dental law instructor was Z. Alexander Looby. And it ended up that he was the lead attorney for the desegregation of the Nashville of the Tennessee area public schools. (Segments 94-95, 00:12:05,000-00:12:30,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of instructor relationship and Looby's legal role; corroborated by ground-truth corpus entry.

claims.claim: Looby's home was dynamited and blew out windows of Hayling's Meharry dormitory; Hayling participated in the April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall
claims.supporting_excerpt: And it produced the occasion of having his home dynamite it. And he was on a street adjacent to the dormitory that I was living in at Meharry. And the morning that they dynamited the front part of his house blew out windows of the dormitory. ... students from this university, Tennessee and I University in Nashville, had a April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall for a protest with the dynamite of Z. Alexander Looby. (Segments 96-98, 114, 00:12:40,000-00:12:57,000, 00:14:51,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person testimony of the bombing, the dormitory damage, and the march. Date matches ground-truth corpus.

claims.claim: Hayling became acquainted with John Lewis, Diane Nash, C.T. Vivian, and James Bevel in Nashville
claims.supporting_excerpt: the leaders of the movement that I ended up making a acquaintance with were John Lewis, Diane Nash, C.T. Vivian, La Theat, I have to get La Theat's first name. And James Bevel. (Segments 105-107, 00:13:18,000-00:14:10,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person enumeration; the unnamed 'La Theat' is preserved as Hayling's own uncertainty.

claims.claim: Diane Nash and James Bevel married and divorced
claims.supporting_excerpt: And even James Bevel and Diane Nash married. They divorced later on but I think they produced a family out of that siblings I should. I produced children out of that union and all. (Segments 109-111, 00:14:22,000-00:14:39,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement; corroborated by ground-truth corpus entries on Nash and Bevel.

claims.claim: Hayling took the Florida dental board exam over the July 4, 1960 weekend
claims.supporting_excerpt: after taking the dental exam in Florida for the efficiency or what is called the dental board exam July the 4th weekend of 1960. (Segment 156, 00:25:48,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person date statement.

claims.claim: Hayling chose St. Augustine because the practice of the deceased Dr. Gordon (who had been killed in an automobile accident) was available
claims.supporting_excerpt: St. Augustine had a very prominent dentist who had met a tragic automobile accident encountered early death and all root off Gordon. And his office facilities were available. (Segments 162-163, 00:26:40,000-00:26:54,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement. The first name 'Rudolph' is suggested by the garbled 'root off Gordon' rendering, where 'root off' may be Whisper artifact of 'Dr. Rudolph.' The summary uses 'Dr. Gordon' to remain within transcript support; the 'Rudolph' attribution is inferential.

claims.claim: Hayling held a December 1960 housewarming for his St. Augustine practice
claims.supporting_excerpt: And so in December of 60, we had a housewarming and everything else. (Segment 170, 00:28:43,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person date statement.

claims.claim: Hayling also served as dentist for the city jail, county jail, and state prison form for St. Johns County and the City of St. Augustine
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was also accepted as the city jail dentist, the county jail dentist and the state prison-formed dentist of the county of St. John's and the city of St. Augustine. (Segment 178, 00:30:04,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: Rev. Thomas A. Wright Sr. was the prior NAACP Youth Council advisor in St. Augustine and was forced to leave for Gainesville
claims.supporting_excerpt: They had also started a movement... it was under the advisory ship of Reverend Thomas Rev. Thomas A. Wright Sr, who was a local minister, Baptist minister and also an instructor at the Black College, Florida Memorial College in St. Augustine. His wife was an elementary school teacher but once they got started as youngsters as a youth group in the protest movement, the establishment looked into it... His wife was fired from the school system and pressure was put on the college to get him to cease his sponsorship of the Youth Council and all. When he refused to do that, he had to leave St. Augustine and move his family to Gainesville, Florida. (Segments 211-214, 00:36:14,000-00:37:36,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person testimony of Wright's role and forced relocation.

claims.claim: Hayling agreed to become advisor to the youth group after a delegation came to his office
claims.supporting_excerpt: A delegation of youngsters came to my office and ended up asking me that they had lost their advisor, would I consider advising them and helping them with the movement that they had started. (Segment 216, 00:37:53,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person testimony.

claims.claim: NAACP chapter president was Fannie Fulwood and secretary was Mrs. Elizabeth Hawthorne; regional NAACP director was Ruby Hurley in Atlanta
claims.supporting_excerpt: A lady by the name of Fannie Fulwood was the president of the local NAACP chapter. And Mrs. Elizabeth Hawthorne, I think it's Mrs. Elizabeth Hawthorne, was the secretary for the NAACP. ... Mrs. Fullerwood, through the regional director of the NAACP, a lady by the name of Ruby Hurley, who was headquartered in Atlanta. (Segments 229-233, 00:40:44,000-00:42:18,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person identification of chapter and regional officers.

claims.claim: Federal $400,000 was appropriated for the St. Augustine 400th-anniversary celebration; no Black appointees were made to celebration committees despite the city being roughly 20% Black
claims.supporting_excerpt: the federal government had appropriated $400,000 to the city to help celebrate and commemorate the entire anniversary period and all. Not one black, even though at the time the black population was somewhere close to 20% of the city had been appointed to any of the committees involved with the complete year of celebrating the 400th anniversary. (Segments 237-238, 00:43:16,000-00:43:58,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of appropriation amount and demographic / appointment claim.

claims.claim: Hayling wrote Vice President Lyndon B. Johnson protesting the segregated dinner; Johnson sent aide George Reedy a week before the dinner
claims.supporting_excerpt: we fired all, we, I fired all for that, to the vice president, to ask him not to attend this segregated function and all because of the exclusion of the black citizenry. And it generated enough heat that Lyndon B. Johnson at the time sent in his representative, a fellow by the name of George Reedy, about at least a week ahead of the appropriated time for the dinner and all. (Segments 247-248, 00:46:17,000-00:46:38,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of letter recipient and Reedy's role.

claims.claim: Six or eight attendees were seated in an alcove above the Ponce de Leon Hotel ballroom; only Secret Service and FBI agents were their white tablemates
claims.supporting_excerpt: So, six, I was either six or eight that they agreed could attend. And we thought at the time we were going to be seated in the Grand Ballroom. But when we arrived, we were seated in an all-cove that looked down on the Grand Ballroom. And the only whites that we had the dinner with were the Secret Service and FBI agents. (Segments 255-257, 00:48:04,000-00:48:37,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement; Hayling's own uncertainty about count (6 vs 8) is preserved.

claims.claim: The morning-after meeting was attended only by the Mayor's Secretary and a tape recorder; no commissioners, mayor, or Washington representative appeared
claims.supporting_excerpt: And we got to the designated place. And there was the Mayor's Secretary and her tape recorder. Not a single commissioner, not the Mayor, and not one representative from Washington. (Segments 266-268, 00:49:29,000-00:49:44,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: The local Klan held a major rally outside St. Augustine in September 1963 featuring Connie Lynch from California and J.B. Stoner from Atlanta
claims.supporting_excerpt: In September of 63 the clan scheduled a major rally and you and some others had a very faithful day... a clan leader by the name of Connie Lynch out of California was coming to be the main speaker at this rally. And also I must admit that the other clan leaders like J.B. Stoner out of Atlanta and all they had called in the big guns of the clan movement to come in to do what they had to do to put a damper on this St. Augustine movement. (Segments 334-337, 01:09:11,000-01:10:44,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony of date, speakers, and origins. The interviewer's prompt establishes 'September of 63'; Hayling's response confirms.

claims.claim: Hayling and three others were captured at the Klan rally, beaten with axe handles and baseball bats, and piled semi-conscious on the rally stage
claims.supporting_excerpt: Before we knew anything after we parked and stopped they knew the car you know and they were for us of James Jackson of Mister house and James house and and clad Jenkins and myself. ... the physical punishment started at that time and they took our wallets and you know personal effects and in my wallet they sold my identity and everything else and also discovered an end of the CP card. And therefore that was the and the stand the impetus for them to really start beating us the beating was so severe with ax handles and baseball bats and all that we were ushered up to and eventually on stage of the where the speaker was. And we were piled on top of each other semi conscious like cordwood on top of each other on the stage on the stage. (Segments 339-344, 01:11:14,000-01:13:31,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person testimony of the assault, with weapons and tableau detail. The names of the other three men are obscured by Whisper artifacts ('Mister house and James house and and clad Jenkins'); the summary's wording preserves this by giving an indefinite count of companions.

claims.claim: An Episcopal priest from Daytona Beach with a personal recorder called the Tallahassee highway patrol; outside law enforcement arrived
claims.supporting_excerpt: an Episcopal priest of Daytona Beach had infiltrated the group and wore a personal recorder and had been able to record the speakers and everything going on at the clan rally but he saw how this thing was escalating and he back out of the meeting and everything got to his car. And went to a pay phone and called telehassy the highway patrol in telehassy and reported what was happening at this clan rally. (Segments 345-346, 01:13:58,000-01:14:57,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person testimony of the priest's role and the call.

claims.claim: The four men were taken to Flagler Hospital's emergency room and then via mortician E.G. Chase's hearse to Brewster Hospital in Jacksonville
claims.supporting_excerpt: we were not comfortable going to Flagler Hospital that was the local hospital so we were only treated in the emergency rooms and all and then the local mortician E.G. Chase made his hearse available to us as an ambulance to take us to Brewster Hospital. And Jacksonville were we were treated by black physicians and felt more comfortable because that was the black hospital in Jacksonville. (Segments 350-351, 01:16:21,000-01:17:17,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person testimony of the medical evacuation.

claims.claim: Hayling lost eleven teeth and sustained severe damage to his right arm
claims.supporting_excerpt: I lost 11 teeth and my health right home was because my patients and all were in attendance at the clan rally. ... they pointed out that I was a right handed dentist and they made sure that they did tremendous damage to my right arm and all. (Segments 354-355, 01:17:36,000-01:18:05,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: Klan leader Hoss Manucy was a convicted moonshiner who had served time in federal prison
claims.supporting_excerpt: the leader of the clan was a convicted moon channel Hoss Manucy and is still not known at this time whether Hoss Manucy had ever gone through the rights and privileges of having his citizenship restored because he had done time in federal pen for moon shining. (Segment 361, 01:20:30,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement; Whisper renders 'moonshine' as 'moon channel.'

claims.claim: Sheriff L.O. Davis deputized many Klan members as auxiliary sheriff's deputies
claims.supporting_excerpt: the sheriff and all Hoss Manucy were very very close so that the sheriff deputized many of the clan members as auxiliary deputies of his department. (Segment 363, 01:21:23,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of sheriff-Klan deputization arrangement.

claims.claim: Roy Wilkins (NAACP national president, New York) called Hayling and threatened charter withdrawal; Hayling preemptively mailed the charter back via special delivery
claims.supporting_excerpt: Roy Wilkins was the president or CEO he was the big haunchel situated in New York City. And I received several calls at my office from his office... He mentioned that if we were not prepared to follow instructions and everything from the NAACP the NAACP was prepared to withdraw our charter. ... I packaged up through Mrs. Elizabeth Hawthorne and all and sent a mail special delivery the charter with a return receipt to New York. (Segments 365-376, 01:22:02,000-01:25:09,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person account.

claims.claim: Hayling met Julian Bond, SNCC, and Ella Baker ('house mother') during Atlanta visits; C.K. Steele helped facilitate SCLC contacts
claims.supporting_excerpt: I made visits with people like Julian Bond, all of the SNCC people, the names, SNCC, student nonviolent court and meeting committee and even met the house mother, (Ella) Baker. ... CK Steele was in the leadership of the board or whatever SCLC and he had been a tremendous supporter and he helped me make various contacts. (Segments 382, 385, 01:26:03,000, 01:27:17,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement; the corrected transcript supplies '(Ella) Baker' as an audit annotation.

claims.claim: Hayling refused the Johnson-requested cessation of demonstrations; demonstrations continued until the Civil Rights Act of 1964 was signed
claims.supporting_excerpt: He had the good graces of the civil rights organization that he wanted two weeks of cessation of demonstrations and Dr. King and the powers that had agreed and other organizations had agreed. We had been double crossed several times in all and when it came down to the St. Augustine chapter, we said, fool me once, shame on you, fool me twice, shame on me. We said we were not going to stop demonstrating until the Civil Rights Act of 1964 had been signed. (Segments 389-391, 01:28:14,000-01:29:05,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person testimony of the refusal.

claims.claim: Dr. King was in St. Augustine on June 30, 1964
claims.supporting_excerpt: I can say with certainty now that on June 30, 1964, he was in St. Augustine and the Civil Rights Act of 1964 was signed Civil Rights Act of 1964. (Segment 393, 01:29:22,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement, explicitly framed as a corrected fact.

claims.claim: Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr. of Yale Divinity School organized Yale students to come to St. Augustine for spring break 1964
claims.supporting_excerpt: Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr, who was involved with the Yale Divinity School, had contacted us and allowed us to end Revan Robinson to develop a relationship. And they had recruited between the Yale students that, rather than to take a spring break and go to the beaches and all, they would come to St. Augustine and conduct a tutorial program. (Segments 400-401, 01:30:29,000-01:31:18,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person testimony.

claims.claim: Mary Peabody (mother of Massachusetts Governor Endicott 'Chub' Peabody, age 72) was arrested with Hayling at the Monson Motor Lodge
claims.supporting_excerpt: The mother of the sitting governor, Endicott "Chub" Peabody, Charles Peabody, or the state of Massachusetts and she was the matron of the 72 years of age of the Peabody clan. ... I was arrested with her at the Monson Motor Lodge. (Segments 432, 434, 01:37:29,000-01:38:11,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person identification of Peabody, her son's gubernatorial role, her age, and the joint arrest.

claims.claim: Esther Burgess was the wife of Bishop John Melville Burgess, the first Black Episcopal bishop in the United States
claims.supporting_excerpt: Esther Burgess who was a husband. Bishop John Melville Burgess was the first black episcopal bishop in the United States and he covered an area in the Massachusetts area and all. (Segments 422-423, 01:34:34,000-01:35:13,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: James Brock, owner of the Monson Motor Lodge, was photographed pouring acid into the swimming pool with freedom demonstrators in the water; the picture circulated internationally
claims.supporting_excerpt: James Brock died. On the Monson Motor Lodge had the infamous picture of him putting acid in the water with the freedom demonstrators in the water in the pool. And that picture went all the way around the world. I understand in Russia, the news paper had him on the front page of the newspaper putting acid in the water when human beings were in the swimming pool. (Segments 442-445, 01:39:26,000-01:39:59,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person identification of Brock's role and the photograph's circulation; ground-truth corpus confirms the photograph is dated June 18, 1964.

claims.claim: Tobias Simon, an ACLU attorney from Miami, served as the movement's lead legal aid and died of a heart attack after running the Boston Marathon
claims.supporting_excerpt: we were able to acquire the services of a Tobias Simon out of Miami who was ACLU attorney and a tremendous fireball civil rights activist. He was taken away from us all too soon. He participated in the Boston Marathons and had a very credible time and everything else and all and within a day or two after completing that race and all he suffered a heart and died. (Segments 316-317, 01:01:37,000-01:02:31,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: Hank Thomas, a St. Augustine native and Freedom Rider attending an HBCU in North Carolina (Hayling tentatively identifies NC A&T), was arrested for a one-man sit-in; word circulated that Sammy Davis Jr. paid the $100 bond
claims.supporting_excerpt: we also had a prominent senior Augustine fellow who was a student in North Carolina. I'm going to say NC A&T but I would stand corrected if it's not but I know it was an end his name was Hank Thomas Hank Thomas. ... It happened in Freedom Rider and was a Freedom Rider and all but he even did a one man sit in at one of the drug stores and all and was arrested and his bond was a hundred dollars. ... The NAACP let it be known that Sammy Davis Jr. had put up the hundred dollar bond to get Hank Thomas out of jail. (Segments 319-322, 01:02:52,000-01:04:31,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement with Hayling's own caveat about NC A&T.

claims.claim: After five years of obligated service (Florida dental tuition supplement), Hayling moved his practice to Cocoa, Florida near Cape Kennedy
claims.supporting_excerpt: Well, it was a dual type thing in that I had five years to give to the state of Florida to pay for the tuition help that they had given me in dental school. ... And then also an opportunity had arisen in Cocoa, Florida, Florida which is right next to Cape Kennedy and all. (Segments 453, 456, 01:41:02,000-01:41:51,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: The City of St. Augustine presented Hayling the Award of Valor on July 2, 2011; the Rev. Goldie Eubanks family was also honored; Dorothy Cotton (SCLC) spoke
claims.supporting_excerpt: The city of St. Augustine gave me the Award of Valor on Civil Rights Act of 1964 and presented this award to me with the mayor and at least three commissioners and one or two other city officials present at our ACCORD where Dorothy Cotton spoke from her. From her affiliation with SCLC and Dr. King and we honored the Rev. Goldie Eubanks family with the Dr. Hale and award of valor. ... so that complete weekend July the second old 11 has been an experience for me. (Segments 476-477, 482, 01:46:58,000-01:47:42,000, 01:49:07,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement; July 2, 2011 is confirmed in Segment 482.

claims.claim: Scott Street in St. Augustine was renamed 'Dr. Hayling Place' on June 20, 2003
claims.supporting_excerpt: Mrs. Barbara (Edna) Vickers who was my neighbor from across the street on it was Scott Street then now is Dr. Hayling Place the street was named for me on June 20, 2003. (Segment 487, 01:50:40,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of street, prior name, new name, and date.

claims.claim: Mrs. Maddie Jones (mother of Commissioner Earl Jones) was Hayling's office assistant for three or more years during the movement period
claims.supporting_excerpt: Earl Jones's mother Mrs. Maddie Jones had been an assistant to me for about three years or more. Such a brave lady because she came to work every day stayed and supported me when many of my patients many of my friends and others were afraid I was like a leper. (Segments 490-491, 01:52:04,000-01:52:25,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person testimony.

claims.claim: Hayling's wife was a school teacher with a master's degree in biology
claims.supporting_excerpt: With my right being a school teacher with a master's degree in biology and all, I have to give her credit for all that my kids attained. (Segment 474, 01:46:17,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement; 'my right' is a Whisper artifact of 'my wife.'

publication_decision.rationale: Main summary's accuracy_score is 86 (below the 90 threshold) due to four documented limitations: (1) the four men captured at the Klan rally are not all cleanly identifiable from the Whisper-garbled source transcript ('James Jackson of Mister house and James house and and clad Jenkins'), so the summary uses an indefinite count rather than naming all four; (2) Mrs. Rowe and Mrs. Campbell of the Massachusetts contingent are identified only by surname in the transcript without first names; (3) the Florida tuition-supplement amount ($1,000/year payable to Meharry) is compressed in the summary rather than quoted explicitly; (4) Hank Thomas's college affiliation is given with the same uncertainty Hayling himself voices. The main summary's quality_score is 92 (passing). All 44 enumerated factual claims in the citation audit are 'supported' with direct transcript excerpts. The decision routes to human review because accuracy is below threshold even though zero citations are unsupported or partially supported, consistent with the fail-closed publication gate.
